metadata.subject: Applied Design, Skills and Technologies
metadata.description: Grade 8 Applied Design, Skills and Technologies introduces students to a range of hands-on disciplines including drafting, electronics, entrepreneurship, food studies, information and communications technology, media arts, metalwork, power technology, textiles, and woodwork. Students develop foundational competencies in applied design thinking, practical skills, and technological literacy while exploring their interests and aptitudes across multiple areas.
metadata.general_description: Integrated core curriculum covering design, practical skills, and applied technologies across multiple disciplines.

big_ideas.statement: The design process provides a systematic approach to solving problems and creating solutions.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students learn that thoughtful planning, iteration, and refinement are essential to creating effective designs and functional products. Understanding how to ask questions, identify constraints, and test solutions builds confidence in tackling complex challenges.

big_ideas.statement: Tools and materials have specific properties and purposes that determine how they can be used effectively.
big_ideas.elaboration: By understanding the characteristics of different materials—wood, metal, textiles, electronics—and the tools that work with them, students can make informed choices about what to use and how to use it safely and skillfully.

big_ideas.statement: Creating with purpose means understanding the end user and making intentional design choices.
big_ideas.elaboration: Whether designing a product, planning a meal, creating media, or building a business idea, students learn to think about who will use or experience what they create and how to make it meaningful and functional.

big_ideas.statement: Technology is a tool that extends human capability and reflects the values and culture of its users.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students explore how digital and physical technologies are created, used, and adapted across different cultures and contexts, recognizing both the benefits and responsibilities that come with technological literacy.

i_can_statements: I can identify a design problem or opportunity and ask clarifying questions about it
I can research ideas and gather inspiration from multiple sources
I can create a simple plan that outlines the steps needed to complete a project
I can set realistic goals and think about potential challenges
I can sketch or model my design ideas to explore possibilities
I can gather feedback from others and use it to improve my design
I can test my design and make adjustments based on what I learn
I can evaluate whether my design meets the original goal or need

competencies.raw: Consider user needs in design decisions
i_can_statements: I can think about who will use what I design and what they need
I can identify accessibility and safety considerations in my design
I can explain how my design choices affect the user experience
I can consider cultural and environmental factors in my design
I can explain my design choices to others clearly
I can create sketches, diagrams, or prototypes to show my ideas
I can present my design and listen to feedback constructively
I can document my design process and decisions

competencies.raw: Select and use appropriate tools and equipment safely
i_can_statements: I can identify the correct tools and equipment for different tasks
I can use tools according to safety guidelines and instructions
I can maintain and care for tools and equipment properly
I can recognize hazards and prevent injuries in the workshop

competencies.raw: Work with materials and demonstrate technique
i_can_statements: I can select materials appropriate for my project
I can apply basic techniques to cut, shape, join, or assemble materials
I can measure and mark materials accurately
I can finish and refine my work to meet quality standards
I can troubleshoot when something doesn't work as planned
I can adapt my approach when faced with unexpected challenges
I can learn from mistakes and apply those lessons to future projects
I can work independently and ask for help when needed
I can work effectively with teammates toward a shared goal
I can contribute ideas and listen to others' suggestions
I can divide tasks fairly and manage timelines
I can reflect on how my group worked together and identify improvements
I can explain the basic components and functions of a technological system
I can identify how inputs, processes, and outputs connect in a system
I can troubleshoot simple technical problems
I can research how technologies are used in different fields

competencies.raw: Apply digital technology and computational thinking
i_can_statements: I can use digital tools to design, plan, and document my work
I can use basic programming or logic to control technology
I can understand how data is organized and used
I can identify opportunities to use technology to solve problems
I can assemble and test simple electronic or mechanical systems
I can combine multiple technologies to create a functional solution
I can debug and refine my technological design
I can explain how my solution works and why I made certain choices
I can identify both benefits and drawbacks of a technology
I can consider how technology affects people, communities, and the environment
I can use technology safely and responsibly
I can think about ethical questions related to technology use

areas.name: Design and Creation
topics: Design thinking and the iterative design process
Design inspiration from nature, culture, and existing products

areas.name: Materials and Techniques
topics: Properties and selection of wood, metal, textiles, and other materials
Safety practices in different workshop environments
Hand and power tool use and maintenance
Sustainable material choices and waste reduction
